message.abstract:
<jats:p>
                  Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-CoV-2) continues to cause significant respiratory disease, particularly in vulnerable populations. Although ACE2 is the primary receptor for viral entry, previous studies have identified a naturally occurring, ACE2-independent entry pathway in certain airway-derived cell lines. Utilization of this pathway depends on surface heparan sulfates and requires the E484D substitution within the receptor-binding domain of the viral Spike (S) protein. In this study, we expand the panel of airway-derived cell lines that support ACE2-independent, S-E484D-dependent replication and identify the host lysosomal transmembrane protein TMEM106B as a critical host factor for infection. Knockout of TMEM106B completely abolishes infection by SARS-CoV-2 S
                  <jats:sup>E484D</jats:sup>
                  in NCI-H522 and NCI-H661 cells. Moreover, ectopic expression of the luminal C-terminal domain (CTD) of TMEM106B-either alone or redirected to the plasma membrane - is sufficient to enable viral entry and infection in otherwise non-permissive cells. We further show that Fc-TMEM106B-CTD decoy protein blocks infection by SARS-CoV-2 S
                  <jats:sup>E484D</jats:sup>
                  , supporting a direct interaction between the S-E484D protein and TMEM106B-CTD. Finally, passaging experiments with a chimeric VSV-SARS-CoV-2 S
                  <jats:sup>E484D</jats:sup>
                  identify additional mutations within the heptad repeat 1 that enhance TMEM106B utilization and viral spread in the ACE2-independent cell models. Together, these findings demonstrate that TMEM106B is a key mediator of a naturally occurring ACE2-independent pathway in multiple airway-derived cells lines and suggest that variation in the Spike protein can expand receptor usage by SARS-CoV-2.
                </jats:p>
title: TMEM106B mediates ACE2-independent replication of the SARS-CoV-2 S-E484D variant in airway-derived cell models
